original_abstract:
# Abstract

The rapid evolution of generative pre-trained transformer (GPT) models has raised significant concerns regarding the misuse of AI-generated content in academic settings, sparking a race to develop effective detection strategies. This study evaluates the efficacy of these detection tools, focusing on their accuracy and the nature of their classification errors. We specifically investigate whether current software can reliably distinguish between human-authored and ChatGPT-generated text, and whether common modifications—such as machine translation and content obfuscation—undermine their performance. 

Our research tests 14 tools, including 12 publicly available detectors and two widely used commercial systems, Turnitin and PlagiarismCheck. The results indicate that current detection technology is neither accurate nor reliable. Most tools exhibit a significant "human-writer bias," frequently misclassifying AI-generated output as human-authored. Furthermore, the effectiveness of these tools drops sharply when faced with content obfuscation techniques. 

This paper provides several key contributions: (1) a comprehensive summary of current scientific and non-scientific efforts in AI detection; (2) one of the most rigorous and extensive comparative tests to date, utilizing an original, controlled document set; and (3) a critical discussion of the practical and ethical limitations of using these tools to police academic integrity. We conclude that current detectors lack the robustness necessary for high-stakes academic decision-making.
humanized_abstract:
# Abstract

Generative pre-trained transformer (GPT) technology has rapidly evolved and there is growing concern of how AI-generated content can be misused within academic settings. The developments of the detection tools have ignited a race, with the aim of creating and developing an adequate mechanism. Our study assesses and discusses the validity, reliability and accuracy of the academic detection tools created in response to the “GPT crisis” and aims to investigate the validity of a model to accurately discern whether the text produced has been generated by a human or by ChatGPT and explore whether there is a considerable reduction in accuracy with modifications to the text, such as machine translation or content obscuration.

We tested 14 tools, including 12 publicly available detectors as well as two commercial plagiarism checkers (Turnitin and PlagiarismCheck). None of the tools we tested provided reliable or accurate results. All but one of the publicly available detectors we tested (and all of the commercial tools) were heavily biased towards human writers, meaning they regularly falsely labelled text generated by AI as having been written by a human. The performance of all the tools degraded dramatically when obfuscation techniques were used.

This paper makes the following contributions: (1) It provides an in-depth survey of the state of the art in AI detection research and practice from both scientific and non-scientific perspectives; (2) It provides one of the most thorough and carefully-designed comparative evaluation efforts to date, using a novel test set of documents; and (3) It reflects on some of the implications of using such tools for enforcing academic integrity. We conclude that today's detection tools are not reliable enough for use in high-stakes academic decision-making.

We tested 14 tools, including 12 publicly available detectors as well as two commercial plagiarism checkers (Turnitin and PlagiarismCheck). All but one of the publicly available detectors we tested (and all of the commercial tools) were heavily biased towards human writers, meaning they regularly falsely labelled text generated by AI as having been written by a human. This paper makes the following contributions: (1) It provides an in-depth survey of the state of the art in AI detection research and practice from both scientific and non-scientific perspectives; (2) It provides one of the most thorough and carefully-designed comparative evaluation efforts to date, using a novel test set of documents; and (3) It reflects on some of the implications of using such tools for enforcing academic integrity. We conclude that today's detection tools are not reliable enough for use in high-stakes academic decision-making.
